<!-- Topic 5: Dynamic Arrays -->
<!-- Total slides: 6 -->

# Dynamic Arrays

---

## What if you don't know the array size until the program runs? {.smaller}

+ Stack arrays require a compile-time size. Dynamic arrays let the size come from the user or a file.
+ `new[]` allocates the array on the heap; `delete[]` releases it.

::: notes
Total slides: 6
:::

<!-- Slide 1 -->

---

## Allocating a dynamic array

```{.cpp}
int n;
cout << "How many scores? ";
cin >> n;

int* scores = new int[n];   // size decided at runtime
```

- `n` does not need to be a constant — any integer expression works
- `scores` is a pointer to the first element, just like a regular array name
- All the pointer arithmetic and subscript syntax from week 13 applies

<!-- Slide 2 -->

---

## Using and releasing a dynamic array

```{.cpp}
for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) {
    cout << "Score " << (i + 1) << ": ";
    cin >> scores[i];
}

// ... process ...

delete[] scores;    // must use delete[], not delete
scores = nullptr;
```

- Use `delete[]` — not `delete` — for arrays allocated with `new[]`
- Mixing them is undefined behavior
- Set to `nullptr` after release

<!-- Slide 3 -->

---

## Complete example

```{.cpp}
#include <iostream>
using namespace std;

int main() {
    int n;
    cout << "How many scores? ";
    cin >> n;

    int* scores = new int[n];

    for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) {
        cout << "Score " << (i + 1) << ": ";
        cin >> scores[i];
    }

    int sum = 0;
    for (int i = 0; i < n; i++)
        sum += scores[i];

    cout << "Average: " << (sum / n) << "\n";

    delete[] scores;
    scores = nullptr;
    return 0;
}
```

<!-- Slide 4 -->

---

## Example — passing a dynamic array to a function {.smaller}

```{.cpp}
#include <iostream>
using namespace std;

int findMax(int* arr, int size);

int main() {
    int n = 4;
    int* vals = new int[n] {15, 8, 42, 23};

    cout << "Max: " << findMax(vals, n) << "\n";

    delete[] vals;
    vals = nullptr;
    return 0;
}

int findMax(int* arr, int size) {
    int max = arr[0];
    for (int i = 1; i < size; i++)
        if (arr[i] > max)
            max = arr[i];
    return max;
}
```

::: notes
A heap array is just a pointer — passing it to a function works identically to passing a stack array. The function signature int* arr, int size is the standard idiom. Ownership (who calls delete[]) stays with main.
:::

<!-- Slide 5 -->

---

## Summary

+ Use `new int[n]` when the array size is not known at compile time.
+ Always pair `new[]` with `delete[]` — and set the pointer to `nullptr` after release.

<!-- Slide 6 -->
